content.problemStatement:
Chef has NNN strings. Each string SiS_iSi‚Äã has length MMM and consists only of lowercase english letters.

Chef likes palindromes, and would like each SiS_iSi‚Äã to be a palindrome. To achieve this, he can perform the following move:


Pick an index iii such that 1‚â§i<N1 \leq i \lt N1‚â§i<N and an index jjj such that 1‚â§j‚â§M1 \leq j \leq M1‚â§j‚â§M.
Then, swap the jthj^{th}jth character of string SiS_iSi‚Äã with the jthj^{th}jth character of string Si+1S_{i+1}Si+1‚Äã.
Informally, Chef can swap the jthj^{th}jth character of any two consecutive strings.

Find the **minimum** number of moves required to make every string a palindrome. Print ‚àí1-1‚àí1 if it is not possible to achieve this.


Input Format

The first line of input contains a single integer TTT, denoting the number of test cases.
Each test case consists of multiple lines of input.

The first line of each test case contains two space-separated integers NNN and MMM, denoting the number of strings and their length, respectively.
The ithi^{th}ith of the next NNN lines contains the string SiS_iSi‚Äã.




Output Format

Print a single integer on a new line as the answer to each test case: the **minimum** number of swaps needed to make every SiS_iSi‚Äã a palindrome, or ‚àí1-1‚àí1 if this is not possible.
constraints: Each SiS_iSi‚Äã contains only lowercase english letters.
The sum of N√óMN\times MN√óM across all test cases won't exceed 5‚ãÖ1055\cdot 10^55‚ãÖ105.

sampleTestCases.explanation:
Explanation:
Test case 111: Chef can make all the strings palindromes in two operations:

First, swap the second characters of S1S_1S1‚Äã and S2S_2S2‚Äã. Now, the strings are {aa,¬Ýbc,¬Ýcb}\{\texttt{aa, bc, cb}\}{aa,¬Ýbc,¬Ýcb}
Then, swap the first characters of S2S_2S2‚Äã and S3S_3S3‚Äã. Now, the strings are {aa,¬Ýbb,¬Ýcc}\{\texttt{aa, bb, cc} \}{aa,¬Ýbb,¬Ýcc}, which are all palindromes.

Test case 222: Both strings are already palindromes.
Test case 333: It can be shown that it's impossible to make all 444 strings simultaneously palindromes.
Test case 444: One optimal sequence of operations is as follows:

Swap the first characters of S3S_3S3‚Äã and S4S_4S4‚Äã. Now, the strings are {abaa,¬Ýbaba,¬Ýaaab,¬Ýbaab}\{\texttt{abaa, baba, aaab, baab} \}{abaa,¬Ýbaba,¬Ýaaab,¬Ýbaab}.
Swap the second characters of S1S_1S1‚Äã and S2S_2S2‚Äã. Now, the strings are {aaaa,¬Ýbbba,¬Ýaaab,¬Ýbaab}\{\texttt{aaaa, bbba, aaab, baab} \}{aaaa,¬Ýbbba,¬Ýaaab,¬Ýbaab}.
Swap the fourth characters of S2S_2S2‚Äã and S3S_3S3‚Äã. Now, the strings are {aaaa,¬Ýbbbb,¬Ýaaaa,¬Ýbaab}\{\texttt{aaaa, bbbb, aaaa, baab} \}{aaaa,¬Ýbbbb,¬Ýaaaa,¬Ýbaab}.

Thus, the final strings are all palindromes. It can be proven that we cannot achieve all palindromes in less than 333 moves.
